(or **meter** in [US spelling](/wiki/American_and_British_English_spelling_differences#-re,_-er "American and British English spelling differences"); symbol: **m**) is the [base unit](/wiki/SI_base_unit "SI base unit") of [length](/wiki/Length "Length") in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI). Since 2019, the metre has been defined as the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of ⁠1/299792458⁠ of a [second](/wiki/Second "Second"), where the second is defined by a [hyperfine transition frequency of caesium](/wiki/Caesium_standard "Caesium standard").[[2]](#cite_note-SIBrochure9thEd-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

SI unit of length

This article is about the unit of length. For other uses of "metre" or "meter", see [Meter (disambiguation)](/wiki/Meter_(disambiguation) "Meter (disambiguation)").

The **metre** (or **meter** in [US spelling](/wiki/American_and_British_English_spelling_differences#-re,_-er "American and British English spelling differences"); symbol: **m**) is the [base unit](/wiki/SI_base_unit "SI base unit") of [length](/wiki/Length "Length") in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI). Since 2019, the metre has been defined as the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of ⁠1/299792458⁠ of a [second](/wiki/Second "Second"), where the second is defined by a [hyperfine transition frequency of caesium](/wiki/Caesium_standard "Caesium standard").[[2]](#cite_note-SIBrochure9thEd-2)

The metre was originally defined in 1791 by the [French National Assembly](/wiki/National_Constituent_Assembly_(France) "National Constituent Assembly (France)") as one ten-millionth of the distance from the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") to the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") along a [great circle](/wiki/Great_circle "Great circle") through Paris, setting 10000 km as that quarter of the [Earth's polar circumference](/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference "Earth's circumference").

In 1799, the metre was redefined in terms of a prototype metre bar. The bar used was changed in 1889, and in 1960 the metre was redefined in terms of a certain number of wavelengths of a certain emission line of [krypton-86](/wiki/Krypton-86 "Krypton-86"). The current definition was adopted in 1983 and modified slightly in 2002 to clarify that the metre is a measure of [proper length](/wiki/Proper_length "Proper length"). From 1983 until 2019, the metre was formally defined as the length of the path travelled by [light](/wiki/Light "Light") in vacuum in ⁠1/299792458⁠ of a [second](/wiki/Second "Second"). After the [2019 revision of the SI](/wiki/2019_revision_of_the_SI "2019 revision of the SI"), this definition was rephrased to include the definition of a second in terms of the caesium frequency Δ*ν*Cs. This series of amendments did not alter the size of the metre significantly – modern measurements of the Earth's polar circumference give a figure of 40007.863 km.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Spelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Spelling")]

*Metre* is the standard spelling of the metric unit for length in nearly all English-speaking nations, the exceptions being the United States[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) and the Philippines[[7]](#cite_note-7) which use *meter*.

Measuring devices (such as [ammeter](/wiki/Ammeter "Ammeter"), [speedometer](/wiki/Speedometer "Speedometer")) are spelled "-meter" in all variants of English.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The suffix "-meter" has the same Greek origin as the unit of length.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The etymological roots of *metre* can be traced to the Greek verb μετρέω (*metreo*) ((I) measure, count or compare)[[11]](#cite_note-11) and noun μέτρον (*metron*) (a measure),[[12]](#cite_note-12) which were used for physical measurement, for poetic metre and by extension for moderation or avoiding extremism (as in "be measured in your response"). This range of uses is also found in Latin (*metior, mensura*), French (*mètre, mesure*), English and other languages. The Greek word is derived from the Proto-Indo-European root *[\*meh₁-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/*meh%E2%82%81- "wikt:*meh₁-")* 'to measure'. [[a]](#cite_note-14) In English, the use of the word *metre* (for the French unit *mètre*) began at least as early as 1797.[[14]](#cite_note-Oxford-15)

## History of definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History of definition")]

This section is an excerpt from [History of the metre](/wiki/History_of_the_metre "History of the metre").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_metre&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Metric_standards_Rijksmuseum.jpg/250px-Metric_standards_Rijksmuseum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metric_standards_Rijksmuseum.jpg)

Replicas of historical metric standards, including an iron copy of the *mètre des Archives*.

During the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), the [traditional units of measure](/wiki/Units_of_measurement_in_France_before_the_French_Revolution "Units of measurement in France before the French Revolution") were to be replaced by consistent measures based on natural phenomena. As a base [unit of length](/wiki/Unit_of_length "Unit of length"), scientists had favoured the [seconds pendulum](/wiki/Seconds_pendulum "Seconds pendulum") (a pendulum with a half-period of one second) one century earlier, but this was rejected as it had been discovered that this length varied from place to place with local gravity. The *[mètre](/wiki/M%C3%A8tre "Mètre")* was introduced – defined as one ten-millionth of the shortest distance from the North Pole to the equator [passing through Paris](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian"), assuming an [Earth flattening](/wiki/Earth_flattening "Earth flattening") of ⁠1/334⁠.[[15]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_Quinn-2012-16)

Following the [arc measurement of Delambre and Méchain](/wiki/Arc_measurement_of_Delambre_and_M%C3%A9chain "Arc measurement of Delambre and Méchain"), the historical French official standard of the metre was made available in the form of the *[Mètre des Archives](/wiki/M%C3%A8tre_des_Archives "Mètre des Archives")*, a [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum") bar held in Paris. It was originally also planned to dematerialise the definition of the metre by counting the number of swings of a one-metre-long pendulum during a day at a [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") of 45°.[[16]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_:1-17) However, dematerialising the definition of units of length by means of the pendulum would prove less reliable than artefacts.[[17]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_Crease-2009-18)[[18]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_:3-19)

During the mid nineteenth century, following the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and the [decolonisation of the Americas](/wiki/Decolonisation_of_the_Americas "Decolonisation of the Americas"), the metre gained adoption in [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), particularly in scientific usage, and it was officially established as an international measurement unit by the [Metre Convention](/wiki/Metre_Convention "Metre Convention") of 1875 at the beginning of the [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution").

The *[Mètre des Archives](/wiki/M%C3%A8tre_des_Archives "Mètre des Archives")* and its copies such as the [Committee Meter](/wiki/Committee_Meter "Committee Meter") were replaced from 1889 by a new standard metre made of [platinum-iridium](/wiki/Platinum-iridium "Platinum-iridium"), and 29 bars calibrated against it were distributed to different nations.[[19]](#cite_note-20) This improved [standardisation](/wiki/Standardisation "Standardisation") involved the development of specialised measuring equipment and the definition of a reproducible temperature scale.[[20]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_la_définition_du_mètre-21)

Progress in science finally allowed the definition of the metre to be dematerialised; thus in 1960 a new definition based on a specific number of wavelengths of light from a specific transition in [krypton-86](/wiki/Krypton-86 "Krypton-86") allowed the standard to be universally available by measurement. In 1983 this was updated to a length defined in terms of the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light"); this definition was reworded in 2019:[[21]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_2019metre-22)

> The metre, symbol m, is the SI unit of length. It is defined by taking the fixed numerical value of the [speed of light in vacuum](/wiki/Speed_of_light_in_vacuum "Speed of light in vacuum") c to be 299792458 when expressed in the unit m⋅s−1, where the second is defined in terms of the caesium frequency Δ*ν*Cs.

Where older traditional length measures are still used, they are now defined in terms of the metre – for example the [yard](/wiki/Yard "Yard") has since 1959 officially been defined as exactly 0.9144 metre.[[22]](#cite_note-History_of_the_metre_Nelson-23)

## SI prefixed forms of metre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: SI prefixed forms of metre")]

Main article: [Orders of magnitude (length)](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(length) "Orders of magnitude (length)")

[SI prefixes](/wiki/SI_prefix "SI prefix") can be used to denote decimal multiples and submultiples of the metre, as shown in the table below. Long distances are usually expressed in km, [astronomical units](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (149,597,871 km), [light-years](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") (63,000 au; 9.5 trillion km), or [parsecs](/wiki/Parsec "Parsec") (210,000 au; 31 trillion km), rather than in Mm or larger multiples. "30 cm", "30 m", and "300 m" are more common than "3 dm", "3 dam", and "3 hm", respectively.

The terms *[micron](/wiki/Micron "Micron")* and *[millimicron](/wiki/Millimicron "Millimicron")* have been used instead of *micrometre* (μm) and *nanometre* (nm), respectively, but this practice is discouraged.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## Equivalents in other units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metre&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Equivalents in other units")]

| Metric unit expressed in non-SI units | | | | | Non-SI unit expressed in metric units | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 metre | ≈ | 1.0936 | [yard](/wiki/Yard "Yard") |  | 1 yard | = | 0.9144 | metre |
| 1 metre | ≈ | 39.370 | [inches](/wiki/Inch "Inch") |  | 1 inch | = | 0.0254 | metre |
| 1 [centimetre](/wiki/Centimetre "Centimetre") | ≈ | 0.39370 | inch |  | 1 inch | = | 2.54 | centimetres |
| 1 [millimetre](/wiki/Millimetre "Millimetre") | ≈ | 0.039370 | inch |  | 1 inch | = | 25.4 | millimetres |
| 1 metre | = | 1010 | [ångström](/wiki/%C3%85ngstr%C3%B6m "Ångström") |  | 1 ångström | = | 10−10 | metre |
| 1 [nanometre](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre") | = | 10 | ångström |  | 1 ångström | = | 100 | [picometres](/wiki/Picometre "Picometre") |

Within this table, "inch" and "yard" mean "international inch" and "international yard"[[24]](#cite_note-25) respectively, though approximate conversions in the left column hold for both international and survey units.

:   "≈" means "is approximately equal to";
:   "=" means "is exactly equal to".

One metre is exactly equivalent to ⁠5 000/127⁠ inches and to ⁠1 250/1 143⁠ yards.

A simple [mnemonic](/wiki/Mnemonic "Mnemonic") to assist with conversion is "three 3s": 1 metre is nearly equivalent to 3 [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)") 3+3⁄8 inches. This gives an overestimate of 0.125 mm.

The ancient Egyptian [cubit](/wiki/Cubit "Cubit") was about 0.5 m (surviving rods are 523–529 mm).[[25]](#cite_note-26) Scottish and English definitions of the [ell](/wiki/Ell "Ell") (2 cubits) were 941 mm (0.941 m) and 1143 mm (1.143 m) respectively.[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) The ancient Parisian *toise* (fathom) was slightly shorter than 2 m and was standardised at exactly 2 m in the [mesures usuelles](/wiki/Mesures_usuelles "Mesures usuelles") system, such that 1 m was exactly 1⁄2 toise.[[28]](#cite_note-H&H-29) The Russian [verst](/wiki/Verst "Verst") was 1.0668 km.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECardarelli2004-30) The [Swedish mil](/wiki/Scandinavian_mile "Scandinavian mile") was 10.688 km, but was changed to 10 km when Sweden converted to metric units.[[30]](#cite_note-31)
